title: The Shark as Provider
summary: A mother and daughter are abandoned by their kin and left to starve. Their fortunes change when a dead seal washes ashore, followed by a talking shark that rises from the deep. The shark promises to protect and provide for them, eventually carrying them and all their possessions on its back to a safe, remote island to escape the predatory Inuarutligaks. This tale highlights unexpected protection from the natural world.
full_text: During a famine that withered the villages of the coast, a mother and her young daughter were abandoned by their extended family. They sat in their hut, weak from hunger. But the sea, which takes as often as it gives, chose that morning to give. A dead seal washed ashore, its body intact and rich with blubber. Then the sea gave something more. From the waves rose a shark, immense and ancient, its skin scarred by centuries. It spoke with a voice like grinding ice. 'I have watched you suffer. Your family has abandoned you, but the sea will not abandon you. I will carry you to a place where the Inuarutligaks cannot find you.' The mother gathered their possessions and climbed onto the shark's broad back. The shark carried them through icy waters for two days and two nights. On the third morning, they reached an island the mother had never seen, rich with birds and seal colonies. 'Here you will be safe,' the shark said. 'The Inuarutligaks cannot cross these waters.' The mother and daughter built a new life on that island. They never forgot the shark, and every year they left offerings at the water's edge in its honor.
moral: The natural world often provides for those whom society has cast aside.

title: The Son of the Sun's Courting in the Land of the Giants
summary: The epic origin myth of the Sámi people. Beaivvi bárdni (the Son of the Sun) sails beyond the easternmost star to the land of the giants to seek a bride. He wins the giant's daughter through strength and cunning. The giant's daughter ties three knots in the wind to help them escape her pursuing brothers—untying each knot releases stronger gales. The brothers are turned to stone by the rising sun. The Son of the Sun marries the giant's daughter on a bearskin and reindeer hide, and she gives birth to the Gállábártnit (the hunting sons), the legendary ancestors of the Sámi people.
full_text: Before the Sámi people walked the fells, before the reindeer knew their migration paths, Beaivvi bárdni—the Son of the Sun—set out on a journey that would shape the world. He sailed beyond the easternmost star, across waters that shimmered with the light of distant suns, until he reached the land of the giants. There, in the shadow of mountains that touched the sky, he sought a bride. The giants roared at his arrival, but the Son of the Sun stood his ground. He proved his strength in contests that shook the earth and his cunning in games that twisted fate. The giant's daughter saw his courage and chose him. When her brothers pursued them across the sky, she tied three knots in the wind—each knot she untied released a gale that pushed the brothers back. As dawn broke, the rising sun turned the pursuing giants to stone. The Son of the Sun married his bride on a bearskin and reindeer hide, and from their union came the Gállábártnit, the hunting sons, the first ancestors of the Sámi. They taught their children to read the wind, to follow the reindeer, and to honor the sun that gave them life.
moral: True strength lies in courage and cunning, not in brute force alone.
source: Anders Fjellner, recorded 19th century; Gaski/Stien translation; laits.utexas.edu

title: The Sun's Daughter
summary: A cycle of myths about Beaivvi nieida, the Sun's daughter, who descended from the sky to teach the Sámi people to tame reindeer, as well as beauty, handicraft, and hunting yoiks. After her death, she was buried on a mountain peak with reindeer neck hair as bedding. Her spirit wanders all of Sápmi with her reindeer herd—visible only when she sleeps. He who discovers her and awakens her with a kiss will gain her and her herd, but must prove himself worthy by following her instructions precisely.
full_text: Beaivvi nieida, the daughter of the sun, grew tired of the cold emptiness of the sky. She descended to earth on a beam of light and walked among the Sámi. She found them struggling through the endless winter, their reindeer wild and untamed. With gentle hands and patient words, she taught them to tame the reindeer, to braid their antlers with ribbons, and to call them with yoiks that rose like smoke from the goahti. She taught the women to sew with sinew thread and the men to carve bone into tools of beauty and precision. When her time on earth ended, she was laid to rest on the highest mountain peak, wrapped in a shroud of njavvi—reindeer neck hair. Her spirit did not leave Sápmi. To this day she wanders with her herd, visible only in the moments between sleeping and waking. The one who finds her and awakens her with a kiss will gain her love and her herd, but he must follow her instructions precisely, for the sun's daughter does not suffer the careless.
moral: Knowledge and skill are gifts from the divine, earned through patience and respect.
source: Anders Fjellner; Marry Áilonieida Somby children's book (Beaivváš nieida); laits.utexas.edu

title: Stállo and Kauras
summary: The classic Sámi Stállo tale. The evil giant Stállo lived in Storfjellet and demanded reindeer sacrifices from the Sámi. He controlled all predators and would kill reindeer if not appeased. The brave Sámi boy Kauras decides to defeat him. After a head-butting contest where Kauras tricks Stállo by hiding his head in a hollowed tree trunk, Kauras survives Stállo's nighttime murder attempt. Terrified, Stállo pays Kauras silver and is expelled from the mountain forever.
full_text: In the shadow of Storfjellet, the Big Mountain, there lived a Stállo so terrible that the Sámi dared not speak his name after dark. He demanded reindeer sacrifices every full moon, and if the offerings stopped, he would send wolves and wolverines to destroy the herds. Kauras, a Sámi boy with fire in his heart, decided that Stállo had taken enough. He climbed the mountain and challenged the giant to a head-butting contest. Stállo laughed, but Kauras had hollowed out a thick tree trunk and hidden it under his hat. When they rammed heads, Stállo reeled in pain while Kauras stood firm. That night, Stállo crept into Kauras's camp and struck him with an axe again and again. But Kauras had placed a log in his bed and slept under a pile of furs. In the morning, Kauras stretched and yawned. 'Stállo, did you feel something? I thought a flea bit me.' Terrified by the boy's apparent invulnerability, Stállo paid Kauras a fortune in silver and fled the mountain forever. Kauras returned to his people rich and celebrated across all of Sápmi.
moral: Brains and courage can overcome a foe many times your size.
source: P. A. Lindholm collection; John Bauer illustration; Barneforlaget.no; Stallo Wikipedia

title: The Stállu's Christmas Meal
summary: A Sámi Christmas legend from Kautokeino. While parents were away at church, a Stállu came to the camp and began killing and eating children one by one. A quick-witted maid saved the youngest child, tricked Stállu with her traveling cape, and escaped on skis behind the fastest reindeer. The reindeer burst into pieces in the Kautokeino River from exhaustion. All reindeer in the camp were turned to stone. The stones remain in the Kautokeino River as a reminder of Stállu's anger.
full_text: In Kautokeino, on a Christmas Eve long ago, the parents traveled to the church far away, leaving the children in the care of a young maid. The fire crackled in the goahti, and the children slept under piles of reindeer furs. Then the door burst open. A Stállu, drawn by the scent of the alone, began his terrible work. One by one, he killed the children and ate them. The maid, her heart pounding, grabbed the youngest child and wrapped him in her traveling cape. She slipped out the back of the goahti and leaped onto the fastest reindeer. They flew across the snow, the Stállu's howls growing fainter behind them. But the reindeer ran so hard that when it crossed the Kautokeino River, its heart burst and its body shattered into pieces. When the parents returned and found the devastation, the father fought the Stállu, who grabbed him by the beard and tore it off. The mother attacked, but the Stállu's rage turned every reindeer in the camp to stone. The stones still stand in the Kautokeino River, a silent monument to that terrible Christmas, a warning to never leave children alone when Stállu walks.
moral: Tragedy etches itself into the land, a warning for those who come after.
source: Skolerom.no (The Sámi Christmas Story about Stállo); Qvigstad collection

title: Sáráhkká: The Childbirth Goddess
summary: Sáráhkká is the most important birth goddess in Sámi mythology, daughter of Máttaráhkká. She lives under the hearth of the goahti. She places the fetus in the woman's womb. She protects both human women and reindeer during birth. Before birth, women drank 'Sáráhkká's booze' and after, ate 'Sáráhkká's porridge.' Wood was split for her to ease delivery. Female animals were sacrificed after successful births.
full_text: Sáráhkká lives beneath the hearth of every goahti, in the warm space where the fire's heat meets the earth. She is the most beloved of the goddesses, the one who holds the power of birth in her hands. Each time a woman conceives, Máttaráhkká gives the child life, and Sáráhkká places it gently in the womb. She stays with the mother through the entire pregnancy, feeling every kick and turn. When the time of birth comes, SáráhkkÁ feels the same pains as the mother. Wood was split in the goahti to ease delivery, each crack of the log helping the birth canal open. Before giving birth, women drank 'Sáráhkká's booze'—a strong herbal brew. After the birth, they ate 'Sáráhkká's porridge' to regain their strength. Sáráhkká protects not only human women but also reindeer during calving. After a successful birth, a female animal was sacrificed in thanks. Christian missionaries tried to replace her with the Virgin Mary, but the Sámi women never forgot Sáráhkká. They whispered her name in the pain of labor and felt her hands guiding the child into the world.
moral: The sacred act of bringing life into the world is guided by divine hands.

title: The Sámi Boy Who Married a Háldi Girl
summary: A Sámi boy falls in love with a háldi girl from the hidden underground people. Despite being from different realms, they find a way to marry. The story explores the boundaries between the visible human world and the invisible world of the háldi, a recurring theme in Sámi folklore. Such marriages come with strict taboos that, if broken, cause the supernatural spouse to disappear forever.
full_text: A young Sámi herder was watching his reindeer on a summer fell when he saw a girl unlike any he had ever seen. She had hair the color of birch bark in sunlight and eyes that seemed to look through him into another world. She was a háldi, one of the nature spirits who live alongside humans but are usually invisible. He fell in love with her, and she with him. They decided to marry, and the háldi girl agreed to live in the human world. But there was a condition. 'You must never,' she said, 'mention my origin. You must never say the word háldi in my presence, or point at me with a metal object. If you do, I will disappear forever.' For years they lived happily. Their children were strong and healthy, their reindeer herd grew. But one day, during an argument, the herder forgot himself. 'You háldi!' he shouted, pointing at her with an iron ladle. She looked at him with sad eyes and began to fade. She disappeared like mist in the morning sun, leaving only the memory of her love and a strict warning: some boundaries should not be crossed.
moral: Love across worlds is fragile; one careless word can shatter what took years to build.
source: Qvigstad & Saba; Sámi Folktales from the Near and Far Worlds; University of Minnesota Press

title: Stuorra-Jouni and the Stállu on Áiligas Fell
summary: A legend from Talvadas on the Teno River. Stuorra-Jouni ('Big Jouni') is a legendary Sámi proto-hero, a wild deer hunter and storyteller. A noaidi from Varanger sent a Stállu to attack Jouni. Jouni met the Stállu on Áiligas fell and they fought according to the ritual pattern. The Stállu escaped on skis, leaving only its ski tracks on the fell, which remain visible to this day.
full_text: Stuorra-Jouni, Big Jouni, was a man of legend on the banks of the Teno River. He was a hunter of wild reindeer and a teller of tales so vivid that listeners forgot to eat. He was too large, too loud, too free for the narrow ways of ordinary men. A noaidi from Varanger grew jealous of Jouni's fame. He beat his drum and summoned a Stállu, sending it across the fells to destroy the great hunter. The Stállu found Jouni on Áiligas fell, where the wind never stops and the view stretches to the ends of the earth. They fought according to the old pattern: first with words, then with challenges, then with weapons. The battle was so fierce that the mountain itself trembled. But the Stállu, realizing it had met its match, turned and fled on skis across the fell. Jouni chased it, but the Stállu was fast. It disappeared into the mist, leaving only its ski tracks carved into the mountain. To this day, the tracks are visible on Áiligas fell, a reminder that Stuorra-Jouni was the first to secure Sámi rights to these lands. Those who see the tracks know that the hero defeated the threat, and the land has been safe ever since.
moral: Great heroes leave their mark on the land, visible to those who know where to look.
source: Journal of Finnish Studies (Talvadas research); scripta.fi

title: The Sacred Sieidi Stones
summary: Legends about sieidis—unusual rock formations considered sacred dwelling places of spirits. Each family had its local sieidi. Offerings of reindeer meat, antlers, and fish were made for hunting luck and protection. The Storjunkare were stones resembling humans or animals, set up on mountain tops. They had power over all animals, fish, and birds. Reindeer were sacrificed at these sites.
full_text: Across the fells of Sápmi, unusual stones rise from the landscape—a boulder balanced impossibly on a smaller rock, a cliff face shaped like a face, a stone standing alone in a hollow. These are the sieidis, the sacred dwelling places of spirits. Each Sámi family knew its local sieidi and made offerings there. A hunter on his way to chase wild reindeer would leave a piece of meat or a reindeer antler. A fisherman would leave a dried fish. A woman hoping for a child would leave a strip of cloth tied to a nearby birch. The Storjunkare were the most powerful sieidis—stones that resembled humans or animals, carefully arranged on mountain peaks or in caves. In winter, the Sámi spread fresh twigs under them. In summer, they spread leaves. The Storjunkare had power over all animals, fish, and birds. A hunter who neglected his offerings would find no game. A family who honored their sieidi would never go hungry. Every clan had its own hill of sacrifice, a place where the bones of reindeer accumulated over generations, a record of respect carved in bone.
moral: Respect for the land and its spirits is the foundation of survival in the Arctic.

title: Sickness Spirits and the Noaidi's Healing
summary: A collection of legends about sickness spirits that cause disease. Illness was understood spiritually—either as soul-loss, as punishment for broken taboos, or as an attack by a hostile noaidi. The noaidi would use his drum to diagnose the cause, then journey in trance to retrieve the lost soul or confront the malevolent spirit. Sacrifices were promised to specific gods if healing was granted.
full_text: When a Sámi fell ill, the noaidi was called. The first task was diagnosis: was the sickness a natural ailment or a spiritual attack? The noaidi beat his drum and read the patterns formed by the reindeer bone on the drum skin. Each symbol told a story. A movement toward Jábmiidáhkká's symbol meant the soul was being pulled to the underworld. A movement toward Radien-attje meant a broken taboo. A movement toward the helping spirits meant an attack by a hostile noaidi. If the soul had been captured by Jábmiidáhkká, the noaidi journeyed to the underworld. He traveled through the three levels of the universe, past the gates of Jábmiidaibmu, and bargained for the soul's return. Sacrifices were promised—a white reindeer, a silver object, a year of service. If the illness was caused by a broken taboo, the noaidi identified the taboo and prescribed the remedy. The patient had to confess the transgression and make amends. If a hostile noaidi had sent a sickness spirit, a battle of spiritual power ensued, each noaidi sending helping spirits against the other. The sick person's fate hung on the outcome of a battle they could not see.
moral: Illness has spiritual roots; healing requires addressing the soul before the body.
source: Emilie Demant Hatt, By the Fire; 'Sickness Spirits' section

title: Snæbjørn (Snow Bear of Suðuroy)
summary: Snæbjørn was a powerful, brave, and proud servant working for a wealthy farmer in Hvalba on Suðuroy. He was secretly in love with a milkmaid. When a Dutch ship illegally traded luxury goods, Snæbjørn bought four silk scarves for her. The judge sentenced him to four years imprisonment on Bremerholm in Denmark for this crime. Enraged, Snæbjørn struck at the judge but accidentally killed the bailiff instead. He fled into the mountains where his speed, climbing skill, and knowledge of the terrain made capture impossible. Villagers secretly helped him with food and warned him of search parties. He became a folk hero—an outlaw who defied unjust Danish authority, reminiscent of Robin Hood.
full_text: Snæbjørn was a powerful, brave, and proud servant working for a wealthy farmer in Hvalba on Suðuroy. He was secretly in love with a milkmaid. When a Dutch ship illegally traded luxury goods, Snæbjørn bought four silk scarves for her. The judge sentenced him to four years imprisonment on Bremerholm in Denmark for this crime. Enraged, Snæbjørn struck at the judge but accidentally killed the bailiff instead. He fled into the mountains where his speed, climbing skill, and knowledge of the terrain made capture impossible. Villagers secretly helped him with food and warned him of search parties. He became a folk hero—an outlaw who defied unjust Danish authority, reminiscent of Robin Hood.
moral: Injustice breeds rebellion.

source_references.title: Färöische Erzählungen und Märchen
source_references.author: V. U. Hammershaimb
source_references.notes: First systematic collection of Faroese oral tradition, including animal legends from Suðuroy

title: Símun of Kirkjubøur (Hulda-Símun and the Hidden Woman)
summary: Símun Símunarson, nicknamed Hulda-Símun or Troll-Símun, was a massive farmer at Kirkjubøur on Streymoy. A hulduman (hidden woman) lived on Kirkjubøur Felltop. The legend tells how Símun was kidnapped by the Huldufólk and taken to the cliffs near Kirkjubøur. After his encounter with the Hidden People, the Huldufólk were never seen in the village again. Símun was described as so large that his jacket required three lengths of double-width cloth, and none of his farmhands dared walk ahead of him on sheep round-ups. The story reflects Faroese beliefs about the Hidden People dwelling in the landscape and their ability to interact with humans.
full_text: Símun Símunarson, nicknamed Hulda-Símun or Troll-Símun, was a massive farmer at Kirkjubøur on Streymoy. A hulduman (hidden woman) lived on Kirkjubøur Felltop. The legend tells how Símun was kidnapped by the Huldufólk and taken to the cliffs near Kirkjubøur. After his encounter with the Hidden People, the Huldufólk were never seen in the village again. Símun was described as so large that his jacket required three lengths of double-width cloth, and none of his farmhands dared walk ahead of him on sheep round-ups. The story reflects Faroese beliefs about the Hidden People dwelling in the landscape and their ability to interact with humans.
moral: Folklore preserves the wisdom of a people.
source: John F. West - Faroese Folk-tales & Legends (1980); The Adventure Seekers

source_references.author: John F. West

title: The Sorceress of Oyndarfjørður
summary: The Rinkusteinar legend in its more detailed form. Long ago, when Irish pirates attacked Fuglafjørður, they killed, robbed, and terrorized the village before setting sail for Oyndarfjørður. An old witch (sorceress) living in Oyndarfjørður saw them coming and rushed to the shore. She raised her arms and cast a powerful curse, transforming the pirate ships into stone. The two rocking stones (rinkusteinar) are the pirate vessels, forever rocking in the waves near Oyndarfjørður, eternally prevented from reaching their destination. The witch's name has been lost to history. The story is one of the best-known witch legends in the Faroe Islands.
full_text: The Rinkusteinar legend in its more detailed form. Long ago, when Irish pirates attacked Fuglafjørður, they killed, robbed, and terrorized the village before setting sail for Oyndarfjørður. An old witch (sorceress) living in Oyndarfjørður saw them coming and rushed to the shore. She raised her arms and cast a powerful curse, transforming the pirate ships into stone. The two rocking stones (rinkusteinar) are the pirate vessels, forever rocking in the waves near Oyndarfjørður, eternally prevented from reaching their destination. The witch's name has been lost to history. The story is one of the best-known witch legends in the Faroe Islands.
moral: Respect the natural world and its boundaries.
source: Superstition Sam - Witches and Wizards of the Faroe Islands; Visit Runavík

source_references.title: Færøiske Folkesagn og Eventyr
source_references.author: V. U. Hammershaimb
source_references.notes: Faroese sorcery legends from the village of Oyndarfjørður, Eysturoy

title: The Sjörå – Lady of the Baltic Sea
summary: The sjörå (sea spirit) is a guardian entity of the Baltic waters surrounding the Åland archipelago. She appears as a beautiful woman, sometimes described as hollow-backed like the skogsrå, who can calm storms or raise tempests depending on how she is treated. Fishermen and sailors traditionally greeted her with respect before setting out. Those who showed her reverence were rewarded with calm seas and good catches, while those who disrespected her faced endless wind and perilous voyages. The sjörå embodies the deep bond between the Åland islanders and the sea that has sustained them for millennia. Stone cairns along the shores of Eckerö, Finström, and Geta mark ancient graves of seafarers — each a monument to lives carried across these treacherous waters. Even today, some islanders speak of her presence when the tide falls silent and the horizon blurs.

source_references.title: Svenska Folksagor
source_references.author: Herman Hofberg
source_references.notes: Swedish water-spirit (sjörå) legends from coastal communities

title: The Storm-Witches of Kastelholm
summary: Åland folklore tells of storm-witches who possessed the secret art of 'tying the wind' into knots of rope. These women could sell sailors a favorable breeze or calm at sea by selling them a knotted rope: untying one knot released a light wind, untying two filled the sails, but untying all three would awaken a devastating tempest. The practice is associated with the maritime culture of Kastelholm Castle, the 14th-century fortress at the heart of the main island. Even today, some islanders refuse to throw rope knots into the sea without cutting them first, for fear of stirring what should sleep. This legend reflects the deep folk belief in weather magic, a tradition found throughout the Nordic coastal regions but with a distinctly Åland character rooted in the archipelago's dependence on safe sea passage.

source_references.title: Ålänningens sagor och sägner
source_references.author: Gunnel Möller
source_references.notes: Collection of Ålandic legends including storm-witch traditions from Kastelholm

title: The Skogsrå – Forest Spirit of Åland's Woodlands
summary: The skogsrå (forest spirit) is a female nature spirit found throughout Swedish-speaking regions including Åland. She appears as a beautiful woman from the front, but seen from behind she has a hollow, rotten back and often a cow's tail or one horse foot. She lures men deep into the forests, leading them astray into swamps or wilderness. Men who follow her may lose their health or sanity after repeated visits. However, she is known to be kind to charcoal burners and woodcutters, and can reward hunters with good fortune. In the Christian reinterpretation common to Åland and the Nordic region, the skogsrå and similar rå spirits were said to be angels who fell from heaven with Lucifer — those who landed in forests became skogsrå, those in water became sjörå. The Åland archipelago, with its blend of pine forests and rocky shores, is considered a natural home for these borderland spirits.

source_references.title: Ålänningens sagor och sägner
source_references.author: Gunnel Möller
source_references.notes: Skogsrå (forest spirit) traditions documented in Ålandic oral lore

title: The Sea Draugr – Ghost of Drowned Sailors
summary: In the Nordic maritime tradition that extends to Åland, the draugr or sea draug is the restless spirit of a drowned sailor. It appears as a figure in oilskins with a head of seaweed, sailing in half a boat. The sea draugr serves as an omen of impending drowning: if a fisherman sees the draug or hears its cries, his own vessel is likely to be lost. The draugr could be placated with offerings — in the old Christmas tradition, beer was left out as 'draug-beer' to keep these spirits from becoming malicious. For Åland, where seafaring has been the backbone of life for thousands of years and where entire communities have lost men to the sea, the draugr legend gave voice to collective grief and the ever-present danger of the Baltic. The tradition of leaving offerings at sea cairns on islands like Eckerö and Geta connects to this belief in appeasing the drowned.

source_references.title: The Supernatural in Medieval Scandinavia
source_references.author: William A. Lindsay
source_references.notes: Academic study of draugr beliefs across Norse and Scandinavian maritime cultures

title: The Saltvik Runestone – Serpent of the Norse Sea
summary: The Saltvik runestone is a second significant Viking-age monument on Åland, located in the Saltvik parish north of Mariehamn. Like the Jomala stone, it is carved with runic inscriptions in the Younger Futhark, its serpentine bands curling like waves around the ancient alphabet. The stone marks the connection between Åland's Viking-era settlers and the wider Norse world, commemorating voyages, kinship, and courage. In local folk belief, runestones were not mere memorials but living objects with spiritual power. Some Åland traditions held that runestones could speak at midnight, or that they protected the land from evil spirits. The Saltvik stone, in particular, is associated with the legend of the Devil's Dance — the parish church at Saltvik being the home of the priest's wife who allegedly danced with the Devil at Bomarsund.

source_references.title: Runes and Runic Inscriptions in the Viking World
source_references.author: James E. Knirk
source_references.notes: Ålandic runestone traditions including serpent imagery from Saltvik

title: Spider Woman and the Origin of Weaving
summary: In the Third World of Navajo tradition, Spider Woman and Spider Man, who lived near the Crossing of the Waters, taught the people the art of weaving. Spider Man showed them how to shape a spindle wheel and spin cotton thread from planted cotton seeds. First Woman declared that spinning must be done toward the body to attract beautiful goods, not away. Spider Man named the spindle 'turning around with beautiful goods' while Spider Woman called it 'turning around with mixed chips.' They constructed a rectangular loom frame, established the warp and weft, and created all parts of the loom. After teaching the people, Spider Man instructed that newborn girls should have spider web rubbed on their hands and arms so they would weave skillfully without tiring.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Separation of Women and Men
summary: In Navajo mythology, First Woman boasted that women could live without men, claiming she thanked her vagina for the deer First Man brought. Enraged, First Man led all men and the two hermaphrodite twins across the river to the north bank. For four years, the sexes lived apart. Women tried to satisfy themselves with bones, feathers, and long stones, while men used animal livers. The people suffered and longed for each other. Owl warned that the separation was wrong and that monsters would be born from it — and indeed, from the women's unnatural acts, monsters were conceived that later terrorized the people. Finally, First Man called across the river, they reconciled, and the people reunited on rafts, resuming their lives together with restored harmony.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Stars and Constellations
summary: First Man, First Woman, Great Coyote, and the Holy People gathered pieces of rock-star mica to create the night sky in Navajo tradition. First Man drew a plan in the dirt to arrange stars like a woven rug. Black God placed the North Star (Sǫʼtsoh). First Man placed the Big Dipper (Náhookǫs bikąʼí) and First Woman the Little Dipper (Náhookǫs baʼáádí). First Man placed the Pleiades (Dilyéhé), which Black God claimed represented parts of his body. They placed Orion's belt (Coyote's Feather), the Rabbit Tracks, and many others. First Angry Coyote, excluded from the careful arrangement, grew impatient and snapped the blanket, scattering the remaining mica chaotically across the sky — explaining why stars appear scattered rather than neatly ordered.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Slaying of Yéʼiitsoh
summary: Monster Slayer's greatest feat in Navajo tradition was killing Yéʼiitsoh, the Big Giant, who wore armor of flint knives on his legs. The Hero Twins approached him after their father the Sun had weakened the giant by striking him four times. Monster Slayer shot an arrow into the giant's leg armor but it shattered; a second arrow broke through. The giant collapsed face-down, and Monster Slayer shot a third arrow through his head. Child of the Water also shot the giant's head. When Yéʼiitsoh began to rise again, Spider Woman warned the twins. Monster Slayer shot a fourth arrow which pinned the giant's hair to the ground. As Yéʼiitsoh died, rivers of blood flowed from his wounds. Monster Slayer used the giant's own stone knife to cut off his head and returned home carrying it as a trophy.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: The Slaying of Déélgééd the Horned Monster
summary: After defeating Yéʼiitsoh, the Hero Twins faced Déélgééd, the Horned Monster, a creatures described as having one horn and living in the water. In some versions the monster was a giant water creature with a single horn. Monster Slayer and Child of the Water tracked it to its lair. Monster Slayer shot the creature multiple times; its blood gushed out and it plunged into the ocean. As it floated on the surface, both twins shot arrows into it until it was dead. The head of Déélgééd floated to the shore where people later used its horn for ceremonies. This victory was one of several that cleared the world of the monsters that had been devouring the people since the time of the separation of men and women.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: The Story of Noqoilpi the Great Gambler
summary: In Navajo tradition, Noqoilpi was a powerful gambler who challenged and defeated all who came to him, taking their possessions and even their freedom. He was said to live at the base of Chʼóolʼį́ʼí (Great Spruce Mountain or El Capitan). Noqoilpi gambled with entire nations, winning their wealth and enslaving their people. The Holy People decided he had grown too powerful and needed to be stopped. They sent a challenger who gambled with Noqoilpi in a series of contests. The challenger, aided by the Holy People, finally beat Noqoilpi at the moccasin game and other gambling games. Defeated, Noqoilpi was forced to leave the Navajo world. He departed eastward, taking his gambling implements with him, and was never seen again, though his influence lingered in the form of gambling as a human vice.
source: Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Story of the Moccasin Game
summary: The Moccasin Game is a traditional Navajo story explaining the origin of a sacred winter game. In the mythological account, the animals gathered to play the moccasin game, which involves hiding a stone or yucca ball under one of four moccasins while the opposing side guesses its location. The game was played between the day animals and the night animals, with the stakes being control over light and darkness. Through trickery and strategy — particularly from Coyote who cheated by using a yucca ring instead of a stone — the night animals won, establishing that darkness would have its place in the world. This story is traditionally told only during winter months and is considered sacred, teaching lessons about balance, trickery, and the boundaries between day and night, summer and winter.
source: Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956); Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: The Sipapu Emergence
summary: In one version of the Hopi emergence story, the people of the Third World grew wicked and corrupt. With the help of Spider Grandmother or bird spirits, a hollow bamboo reed grew at the opening between the Third World and the Fourth World. This opening, called the sipapu, is traditionally located at the Grand Canyon. Only the people with good hearts — those who showed kindness — were able to make the journey through the sipapu to the Fourth World, the world of the present. In the Fourth World, they encountered Masauwu, the Spirit of Death and Earth God, who wore a hideous but also beautiful mask. Masauwu gave them their land and four sacred tablets outlining their way of life and their future wanderings. He taught them to worship properly and ensured the dead would return safely to the underworld.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis

title: Sikakokuh and the Hunting Dog
summary: A Hopi story about a boy named Sikakokuh who had a remarkable hunting dog. The dog was no ordinary animal but possessed spiritual power, able to track game without fail and communicate with its master. Sikakokuh's success as a hunter made others jealous. Through the dog's guidance, Sikakokuh learned the proper rituals for hunting, including prayers and offerings to the spirits of the animals he killed. When enemies or jealous rivals tried to harm the dog, supernatural forces protected both boy and animal. The story teaches the sacred relationship between humans and animals, the importance of gratitude, and the proper conduct of hunters who must respect the spirits of the animals they take.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: The Serpent of the Sea (Kolowisi)
summary: A Zuni legend about Kolowisi, the great horned water serpent that lives in the sacred lake. A maiden from Zuni went to the lake and was seen by Kolowisi, who took her as his bride beneath the waters. She lived with the serpent in his underwater home, where there was no light but she was treated well and given abundance. The people mourned her loss and held ceremonies to bring her back. Eventually, Kolowisi allowed her to return to the surface, but she was transformed and carried spiritual power from her time in the serpent's realm. She brought knowledge of healing ceremonies and water-songs back to the Zuni people. Kolowisi is generally a benevolent figure, associated with water, fertility, and the life-giving forces of the natural world. The serpent's horn symbolizes power, and offerings are made to Kolowisi during ceremonies asking for rain.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901); various Zuni oral traditions

title: The Seven Sacred Rites
summary: White Buffalo Calf Woman gave the Lakota people the Seven Sacred Rites that would guide their spiritual life. The rites are: 1) Keeping of the Soul (preserving the spirit of the deceased for a year before release); 2) The Purification Rite (sweat lodge ceremony for cleansing); 3) Crying for a Vision (vision quest for guidance); 4) The Sun Dance (annual renewal ceremony with sacrifice and dancing); 5) The Making of Relatives (adoption ceremony); 6) The Girls Coming of Age (womanhood ceremony); and 7) The Throwing of the Ball (symbolizing the Creator's love). Each rite uses the sacred pipe given by the White Buffalo Calf Woman.

title: Sweet Medicine and the Four Sacred Arrows
summary: Sweet Medicine (Motsé'eóeve) was the great prophet and law-giver of the Cheyenne people. He journeyed to Nóávóse (Bear Butte) in the Black Hills where Ma'heo'o (the Wise One Above) gave him the four sacred arrows—the Maahótse. Two arrows were for hunting, to ensure success in finding buffalo, and two were for warfare, to protect the people from enemies. Sweet Medicine also received the structure of Cheyenne government, the Council of Forty-Four peace chiefs, the military societies, and the system of legal justice. He predicted the coming of horses, white men, and the loss of the buffalo. He also invented the hoop-and-stick game (chunkey).

title: Skunk Outwits Coyote
summary: Coyote came upon a prairie dog village and decided to catch himself a meal. He blocked all the holes except one and waited with his mouth open at the entrance. But Skunk saw what Coyote was doing and decided to teach him a lesson. Skunk approached Coyote and asked what he was doing. Coyote explained his plan, and Skunk offered to help by driving the prairie dogs toward Coyote's mouth. Instead, Skunk walked up to Coyote and sprayed him directly in the face. Coyote ran away howling, and the prairie dogs escaped. Skunk laughed at the trickster, proving that even the cleverest schemer can be outsmarted.

title: The Sweat Lodge Origin: Seven Bison and Bones Together
summary: Seven Bison and their male human companion fought against the angry bison Bones Together, whose pure bone armor was impenetrable. As each bison charged, they broke their legs against Bones Together. Finally the human companion, blessed by an eagle with the power to transform into a feather, faced Bones Together. The monster charged four times, but the man turned into a feather and wafted out of harm's way. On the fourth charge, the man shot an arrow into Bones Together's rectum, piercing his heart. Though victorious, the bison were grievously injured. Together they performed the first Sweat Lodge ceremony, which healed all the bison. Afterward, the Seven Bison rose into the sky to become the Big Dipper, and the man became the Little Dipper.

title: The Sun Dance Lodge Symbolism
summary: The Crow Sun Dance lodge (Ashé Isée) is built as a microcosm of the universe. The central forked pole, representing God, faces east. A white flag tied to one branch represents Earth; a blue flag represents the sky. Three charcoal rings on the pole symbolize the three days of the dance and the three worlds of Crow cosmology. A buffalo head and eagle body are hung on the pole as patrons. Twelve cottonwood posts form the lodge boundary, representing the twelve months and the circle of life. For contemporary Christian Crows, the twelve posts also symbolize the twelve apostles and the three rings represent Christ's three days in the tomb.

title: The Spring Awakening Ceremony
summary: The most important Pawnee ceremony was the Spring Awakening, meant to awaken the earth and prepare it for planting. The timing was determined by celestial observation: the priest would track 'two small twinkling stars known as the Swimming Ducks in the northeastern horizon near the Milky Way.' When these stars appeared and the first rolling thunder was heard from the West, the ceremony began. The ritual involved songs, prayers, and the renewal of sacred bundles. The Skidi Pawnee performed this as the Thunder ceremony, focusing on corn renewal, while the South Bands performed the White Beaver ceremony, which revitalized hibernating animals.

title: Splinter Foot Girl
summary: A childless Arapaho woman found a splinter embedded in her foot. To her amazement, the splinter grew and transformed into a baby girl. The girl grew rapidly and possessed supernatural abilities. She could communicate with animals and had the power to heal. She brought good fortune to her family and community, helping them find buffalo and warning them of danger. One day, a rival tribe kidnapped her, but she used her powers to escape and return home. The story teaches that blessings can come from unexpected places, and that even something as small as a splinter can hold great potential.

title: The Sun Dance Wheel
summary: The Arapaho Sun Dance, known as the Offerings Lodge, is a central spiritual ceremony. Unlike other Plains tribes that practiced self-torture, the Arapaho version involved donating personal items, space, or land to the community as sacrifice. The sacred Flat Pipe was central to the ceremony, symbolizing the Arapaho's connection to the Creator. The Sun Dance lodge was built with a central pole representing the Creator, surrounded by a circle of posts representing the community. The ceremony lasted four days and included dancing, prayer, singing, and offering. The wheel symbolism represents the cycle of life and the interconnectedness of all things.

title: The Star Husband (Arapaho)
summary: Two Arapaho women lay outside their tipi at night. One pointed at a bright star and said she wished to marry it. The next day, a handsome young man appeared at their camp. He was the star in human form, come to claim his bride. The woman went with him to the sky world, where she lived among the star people. But she grew homesick and was told she could return to Earth if she did not eat certain food. She broke the taboo and immediately found herself back on Earth. Her star husband appeared no more, but she gave birth to a son who became a legendary Arapaho hero.

title: Shakuru and Pah: The Sun and Moon
summary: Shakuru (the Sun) and Pah (the Moon) were the last of all gods placed in the heavens by Tirawa. They are of relatively minor standing in Skidi Pawnee mythology compared to the star gods. The Sun and Moon produced a son, a boy who was placed on Earth to be the ancestor of the Pawnee people. The Sun is associated with warmth, light, and the daily cycle. The Moon governs the night and is associated with women's cycles. In Pawnee earth lodge construction, the door faces east to welcome the rising sun, and the smoke hole is aligned to observe the moon and stars.

title: The Star Woman (Blackfoot)
summary: A Blackfoot chief's daughter married a star and went to live in the sky world. Each day she was given the task of digging roots, but she was forbidden from digging up one particular turnip. Curiosity overcame her, and when she pulled the turnip, she saw the Earth far below. Overcome with homesickness, she wove a rope of sinew to lower herself back. But the rope was too short and she fell, landing safely but finding herself alone. Following a stream, she found her way back to her people, forever changed by her time among the stars. Her story became a lesson about obedience and the bond between Earth and sky.

title: The Sacred Pipe (Blackfoot)
summary: The Blackfoot believe the sacred pipe was given to them by the Thunder spirit, who taught them the songs and rituals for its use. A man once heard thunder and saw a lightning-struck tree. Where the lightning had struck, he found a beautifully carved pipe. The Thunder spirit spoke to him, instructing him to form a society of pipe keepers. The pipe would be used for healing, peace-making, and communicating with the spirit world. The Blackfoot Medicine Pipe ceremony involves elaborate rituals, the smoking of the pipe, and the offering of prayers to Apistotoke for the well-being of the people.

title: The Stone Giants (Otneyarhed / Stone Coats)
summary: The Stone Giants, also known as Stone Coats, were a race of cannibalistic giants whose skin was as hard as stone, making them nearly invulnerable to weapons. They were the mortal enemies of humans and the constant antagonists in Iroquois hero tales. According to legend, they were exterminated through the cunning and bravery of heroes who discovered that their only weakness was the soft spot on the top of their heads, where a special stone or arrow could pierce their otherwise impenetrable hides. Tales include the Stone Giant's wife, who helped humans, and the story of a young warrior who challenged a Stone Giant in personal combat. Their threat was so great that it required the intervention of the Thunderers to finally drive them away.
source: Bruchac, Joseph - Stone Giants & Flying Heads; Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; native-languages.org

title: The Stone Giant's Wife
summary: This legend tells of a human woman who was taken captive by a Stone Giant. Against all expectations, she survived in the giant's lodge by learning his habits and discovering the secret weakness of his kind — the soft spot on the crown of the head. She eventually escaped and returned to her people with the critical knowledge that would allow them to defend themselves against the stone-coated monsters. The story reflects the Iroquois belief that even the most terrifying adversaries have vulnerabilities, and that courage and observation can overcome seemingly insurmountable threats. It was one of the most popular tales told around winter fires, and versions appear in the early ethnographic collections of the Bureau of American Ethnology.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; Bruchac - Stone Giants & Flying Heads

title: The Stone Giant's Challenge
summary: A heroic Iroquois warrior once issued a challenge to a fearsome Stone Giant who had been terrorizing villages. The giant accepted and the two met in battle. The warrior discovered that ordinary weapons could not pierce the giant's stone skin. Using his wits, he lured the giant onto treacherous ground where a rockslide buried him, then climbed to the giant's head and found the vulnerable soft spot. This story was recorded in multiple versions by anthropologists in the 19th century, including by Erminnie Smith among the Onondaga and by Arthur C. Parker among the Seneca. It belongs to a cycle of legends about the extermination of the Stone Giants, which the Iroquois believed once ruled the land before humans were created.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; Parker, Arthur C. - Seneca Myths and Folk Tales (1923)

title: Spearfinger (U'tlun'ta): The Stone Witch (Cherokee)
summary: Spearfinger, also known as Nûñ'yunu'ï or 'Stone-dress,' is a fearsome Cherokee monster and witch who lives along the eastern side of Tennessee and western North Carolina. She has a long, sharp forefinger on her right hand like a spear or obsidian knife, which she uses to cut open her victims. She wears a dress made of stone that makes her nearly invulnerable. Spearfinger feeds on human livers, especially those of children. Her mouth is perpetually stained with blood. She can mimic the voices of loved ones to lure victims close. According to legend, she was eventually defeated by a group of Cherokee warriors who discovered that her only vulnerable spot was a small point on her palm. They shot arrows into her palm, and she fell from the mountain where she lived.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Sacred Texts - U'tlun'ta

title: Stone Coat (Nûñ'yunu'ï): The Rock Giants (Cherokee)
summary: Stone Coat, also called Nûñ'yunu'ï, is a race of giant beings covered in stone-like skin that makes them nearly invulnerable. They dwell in the remote mountains and caves of Cherokee territory. These rock giants are cannibalistic and prey upon humans who venture too far into their domain. They are incredibly strong, capable of throwing boulders and tearing down trees. Stone Coat is also associated with Spearfinger, who wears a stone dress. The giants' only weakness is their vulnerability to certain spiritual practices. Cherokee medicine men knew rituals to confuse or drive away Stone Coats. The legend may have been inspired by encounters with the fossilized remains of prehistoric megafauna in the Appalachian region, and it serves as a warning against venturing into dangerous, unknown territory.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Stoneclad

title: Sinti Lapitta: The Horned Serpent (Choctaw)
summary: Sinti Lapitta is a great horned serpent from Choctaw mythology, a water monster with immense power. Described as a giant snake with a head like a panther or with two horns on its head, it dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and water holes. Unlike the malevolent horned serpents of some tribes, Sinti Lapitta was sometimes known to visit unusually wise young men, bestowing knowledge and power upon them. The serpent was both feared and respected, embodying the power of the water realm. It served as a symbol of the underworld and was associated with the chaotic forces that must be balanced against the order of the sky. Stories of Sinti Lapitta warn against disturbing sacred waters.

title: Seminole Creation: Breathmaker and the Earth (Seminole)
summary: The Seminole creation story begins with Breathmaker, the great Creator god, who existed before anything else. Breathmaker created the earth and all living things. In the Seminole version of the Muskogean creation tradition, the earth was formed from mud brought up from the primordial waters, similar to the Cherokee story. Breathmaker then created humans, animals, and plants, and taught the Seminole how to live properly. He gave them the Green Corn Ceremony and established the clans. Breathmaker also created the sun, moon, and stars to govern time and seasons. The Seminole believe that Breathmaker remains involved in the world and can be communicated with through prayer and ceremony. The creation story establishes the sacred relationship between the Seminole people and their land in Florida.

title: Stikini: The Owl Witches (Seminole)
summary: Stikini are dreaded owl-witches from Seminole folklore. During the day, Stikini appear as ordinary humans, but at night they transform into monstrous owls with supernatural powers. They are known to prey on humans, particularly those who are alone in the forest at night. A Stikini can be identified by its bloodshot eyes and unusual behavior. The owl-witches are associated with witchcraft and dark magic, and their appearance is considered an omen of death or misfortune. Seminole medicine men have the power to identify Stikini and perform rituals to protect against them. The legend serves as a warning against practicing evil magic and emphasizes the importance of staying within the protection of the community, especially at night.

title: Sint-Holo: The Horned Serpent (Chickasaw)
summary: Sint-Holo is a dragon-like horned serpent from Chickasaw mythology. This powerful water monster dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and underground springs. Sint-Holo has a serpentine body covered with scales, a horned or antlered head, and possesses supernatural powers. Unlike some malevolent serpents in neighboring tribes' mythology, Sint-Holo is sometimes ambivalent or even protective of the Chickasaw people. The serpent is associated with the underground water realm and represents the forces of nature that must be respected. Those who encounter Sint-Holo must show proper respect or face dire consequences. The horned serpent symbol appears in Chickasaw art and ceremonial objects and is one of the most widespread mythological figures across Southeastern Native American cultures.

title: The Sea Wolf (Wasgo) (Haida)
summary: Wasgo the Sea Wolf is a powerful supernatural being in Haida mythology, a creature that combines the features of a wolf and a whale. He possesses the strength of both land and sea animals and serves as an important crest figure for Haida clans. In the narratives, Wasgo descends from a supernatural lineage and mediates between the ocean and forest worlds. He appears in Haida art as a composite creature with a wolf's head and body but with the fins and tail of a killer whale, often depicted with exaggerated features indicating supernatural power. Wasgo stories teach about the fluid boundaries between animal species in the Haida worldview and the power that comes from understanding both the land and sea. The sea wolf is considered one of the most potent supernatural helpers a person could receive.
source: Haida oral tradition; Haida art and crest narratives

title: Sisiutl the Double-Headed Serpent (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: The Sisiutl is a fearsome three-headed sea serpent whose glance can turn an adversary into stone. Typically depicted as a double-headed serpent with a central humanoid face representing Baxbaxwalanuksiwe' or Komokwa, it has horned heads, protruding tongues, and spiral noses that indicate supernatural power. The Sisiutl is closely associated with shamans, who mediate between natural and supernatural worlds, and warriors believed that its blood made them invincible in battle. It appears as a protective figure on clan house beams, war canoes, and ceremonial regalia. In some myths, the Sisiutl transforms into an invincible war canoe or a magic belt that protects the wearer against all dangers. The beach mineral mica was believed to be the scales of the Sisiutl and was incorporated into ceremonial traditions. The Sisiutl plays a dual role as both a terrifying monster and a powerful helper to ancestors who earned its favor.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Franz Boas; Wikipedia

title: The Story of Asdiwal (Tsimshian)
summary: Asdiwal is one of the most important Tsimshian hero narratives, recorded in multiple versions by Franz Boas. The story begins with a mother and daughter dying of starvation during a winter famine in the Skeena River region. A supernatural being named Hatsenas (a bird of good omen) appears, provides food, and fathers Asdiwal with the younger woman. Hatsenas gives his son magical objects including a never-missing bow, invisible-making hat, and inexhaustible food basket. Asdiwal's adventures include hunting a magical bear up to the sky, where the bear transforms into Evening-Star, daughter of the Sun. He marries her after passing deadly trials set by the Sun. Returning to earth, Asdiwal marries a Tsimshian chief's daughter and becomes wealthy through potlatches. He competes with his brothers-in-law in sea-lion hunting but is abandoned on a reef during a storm. His father saves him. Eventually Asdiwal goes mountain hunting without his snowshoes, gets lost, and is turned to stone with his dog and lance — a transformation that can still be seen on a mountain at Ginadâos Lake.
source: Franz Boas, Tsimshian Texts (1895, 1902, 1912); Tsimshian oral tradition recorded by Henry Tate

title: The Story of Ista (Nuxalk)
summary: Ista is the name of the First Woman who descended to Nuxalknalus (King Island) in Nuxalk tradition. She came down to earth and decorated her crest on a blanket with abalone shell and danced up the coast. Ista was greatly admired for how she chose to show her connection to the Creator. Her story is part of the Nuxalk understanding of how the first ancestors established territories and rights to the land. Traditional family names are handed down and validated at potlatches. The Ista story also includes the tale of Patsallht (First Born), who thought he was the only one on earth. He was given a medicine box containing a 'mukw' of shame, stubbornness, and jealousy. He met the Sisawk people (Society of Chiefs) but was ashamed he was not a member. Later, he met a beautiful woman wearing a red blanket who proposed marriage. She was a grizzly bear woman who built him a home from hemlock. When she left him, First Born refused to leave the house. People followed his puppy to find him and convinced him to feast with them by offering to paint their faces black. The Ista people were ultimately wiped out by a cannibal raiding party from far away.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; Skwucwlikwana (Lucy Mack); Nuxalk Nation

title: The Sun Tests His Son-in-Law (Nuxalk)
summary: In this Nuxalk myth, the daughter of the Sun married a human man known as Salmon Boy. The Sun was not pleased with his daughter's choice and subjected his new son-in-law to a series of deadly tests. The Sun took Salmon Boy to a mountain of unparalleled steepness where mountain goats climbed, commanding him to kill one. Salmon Boy succeeded with the help of his wife. Next, the Sun required him to draw water from a spring whose walls closed in on anyone who approached. Again his wife helped him. The Sun tried to bake him in a supernaturally hot sweat lodge, burn him on hot stones, and crush him with a falling tree. Each time Salmon Boy survived through his wife's guidance and his own growing supernatural power. Finally recognizing his son-in-law's worth, the Sun accepted him and bestowed upon him the gift of bringing salmon to the people. Salmon Boy returned to earth as a powerful being who taught the Nuxalk people how to properly catch, prepare, and honor the salmon, establishing the sacred relationship between the people and the salmon runs.
source: Franz Boas, The Mythology of the Bella Coola Indians; Nuxalk oral tradition

title: The Slave Who Became a Buzzard
summary: A Gullah version of the Flying Africans legend, recorded by the Federal Writers' Project in the late 1930s. Wallace Quarterman, a Gullah man born in 1844 on Sapelo Island, Georgia, recounted that the Igbo people who drowned at Dunbar Creek did not die at all. According to the oral tradition passed down through generations, the Igbo 'rise out of the water and turn into buzzards and fly back to Africa.' In Gullah belief, the buzzard is not a carrion eater but a sacred bird that carries the souls of the dead back across the Atlantic. This story reflects the deep spiritual connection between the Gullah people and their West African ancestors, and the belief that death by water was not an end but a transformation. The buzzard's circling flight over the Georgia coast is seen as the lingering presence of those who refused to be slaves, forever watching and waiting.
source: Federal Writers' Project Slave Narratives (1930s); snip Wallace Quarterman interview

title: Stagolee Shoots Billy Lyons
summary: On Christmas night, 1895, in a St. Louis saloon, the notorious bad-man Stagolee (based on historical figure Lee Shelton) shot William 'Billy' Lyons after a dispute. In the folk tradition, the argument began when Billy Lyons snatched Stagolee's prized Stetson hat from his head and refused to return it. Stagolee warned him: 'I don't care what you do with me, but don't you mess with my Stetson hat.' When Billy laughed and refused, Stagolee drew his .44 pistol. Billy begged for his life, pleading that he had a wife and children, but Stagolee replied, 'I don't care about your wife and kids—you should have thought of them before you touched my Stetson hat.' He shot Billy dead. The song spread like wildfire across America, with each new version making Stagolee more mythic, transforming a common barroom murder into a foundational American outlaw legend—the quintessential 'bad nigger' who answered to no man and no law.
source: Historical record (St. Louis Globe-Democrat, 1895); snip African American folk ballad tradition

title: Stagolee in Hell (Stagolee vs. the Devil)
summary: After Stagolee was hanged for murder, he arrived at the gates of Hell, still wearing his Stetson hat and carrying his .44 pistol. When the Devil tried to send him to his eternal punishment, Stagolee refused to submit. He pulled his pistol and challenged the Devil himself. The Devil, for all his power, found he could not control Stagolee. Stagolee kicked the Devil off his throne, sat down on it himself, and took over the running of Hell. In some versions, he set all the lost souls free; snip in others, he ran Hell more efficiently than the Devil ever did. The Devil eventually begged God to let Stagolee into Heaven, but Heaven refused as well, leaving Stagolee master of his own domain. This tale of a man so bad that Hell itself could not hold him became a cornerstone of African American 'toasts' (narrative poems), celebrating the ultimate rebellious figure who fears neither God nor Devil and answers to absolutely no one.
source: African American toast tradition; snip Julius Lester, Black Folktales (1969); snip folk ballad variants

title: Stagolee's Funeral
summary: The folk ballad describes Stagolee's funeral as an event unlike any before seen. Thousands of people came from miles around to see the baddest man who ever lived laid to rest. A procession of mourners stretched for blocks—the women weeping, the men silent and respectful. His coffin was reportedly so fine it cost more than most men earned in a year. The ballad describes the funeral train, the flowers, the hymns sung over his grave. Yet even in death, Stagolee remained defiant. The women who heard of his death 'could not stay at home,' rushing to the funeral in droves. Verses describe how 'the women in the West' heard the news and lamented, cementing Stagolee's status as a tragic romantic figure. Stagolee's funeral became a set-piece in African American folk memory: the moment when the community paused to honor a man who, for all his violence, represented a kind of freedom—the freedom to be utterly oneself, without apology or compromise, in a world that demanded constant submission from Black men.
source: African American folk ballad 'Stagger Lee' (multiple collected variants)

title: The Sea Island Creation Frog
summary: In Gullah Geechee creation folklore, the frog was one of the first creatures and played a crucial role in shaping the Sea Islands. The story tells that in the beginning, there was only water everywhere. The Creator needed someone to dive to the bottom and bring up mud to make land. All the big animals tried and failed—they could not hold their breath long enough to reach the bottom. Finally, the little green frog volunteered. The big animals laughed at him, but the frog dove down, down, down, past the fish, past the whales, to the very bottom of the ocean. He scooped up a mouthful of mud and kicked his way back up. When he surfaced, the Creator took the mud from the frog's mouth and formed the Sea Islands. As a reward, the frog was allowed to live both in water and on land and to sing the loudest song of any creature at dawn. This pourquoi tale explains both the origin of the Gullah homeland and why frogs sing so loudly at daybreak, connecting the Gullah people's deep sense of place with the natural world around them.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Sea Islands folklore collections

title: Santa Muerte — Holy Death
summary: Santa Muerte (Holy Death or Saint Death) is a female folk saint and personification of death venerated primarily in Mexico and the US Southwest, with an estimated 12 million followers worldwide. She is depicted as a female skeleton wearing a long robe and carrying a scythe and a globe. Her origins blend pre-Columbian death deities such as the Aztec goddess Mictecacihuatl (Lady of the Dead) with the Spanish Grim Reaper (la Parca). Devotees pray to Santa Muerte for protection, healing, financial prosperity, love, and safe passage to the afterlife. Her statues are dressed in different colored robes depending on the petition: white for purification, red for love, gold for money, black for protection against enemies, green for justice. Though condemned by the Catholic Church, Santa Muerte's following exploded in the early 2000s and spread rapidly to the United States through Mexican immigration. Her shrines are found in botanicas and homes throughout Los Angeles, Houston, Chicago, New York, and other cities with large Mexican-American populations. She has particular appeal among those living on society's margins — taxi drivers, sex workers, police, soldiers, and prisoners — who face death's uncertainty daily.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Muerte; Chesnut, R. Andrew 'Devoted to Death: Santa Muerte, the Skeleton Saint' (2018)

title: Shango and the Dual Death (Yoruba)
summary: Shango was the fourth king of Oyo who deified himself as the god of thunder and lightning. According to legend, Shango was a powerful but tyrannical ruler who accidentally destroyed his own palace with lightning when his enemies provoked him. Overcome with grief and shame, he fled Oyo and hanged himself from an ayan tree. But rather than dying, Shango ascended to heaven and became an Orisha of immense power. His two wives, Oshun and Oya (who became the goddess of wind and death), followed him into the spirit world. Shango's sacred symbol is the oshe (double-headed axe), representing swift divine justice. His followers revere him for his fiery temper, his drumming, and his power to hurl thunderbolts at his enemies.

source_references.title: Flash of the Spirit: African & Afro-American Art & Philosophy
source_references.author: Robert Farris Thompson

title: The Serpent Rainbow Ayida-Weddo (Fon/Dahomey)
summary: Ayida-Weddo is the rainbow serpent, the androgynous cosmic serpent who supports the world. According to Fon cosmology, the universe is held up by Ayida-Weddo, who coils beneath the earth and stretches into the sky as the rainbow. When the serpent moves beneath the ocean, it causes earthquakes and tidal waves. Ayida-Weddo is also known as Damballa in Haitian Vodou, where it is revered as the ancestral spirit of wisdom and the connection between heaven and earth. The serpent sheds its skin as a symbol of rebirth and eternal renewal. In Dahomean art, Ayida-Weddo is often depicted as a double rainbow serpent, representing the union of the celestial and earthly realms. Offerings of white cloth and eggs are made to appease the great serpent.

source_references.title: A Treasury of African Folklore: The Oral Literature, Traditions, Myths, Legends, Epics, Tales, Recollections, Wisdom, Sayings, and Humor of Africa
source_references.author: Harold Courlander

title: The Story of Saint Yared and the Birth of Ethiopian Music
summary: In the 6th century, a young boy named Yared was sent to study at the holy city of Axum. But he was a slow learner and often ran away to play in the fields. One day, while hiding under a tree, Yared saw a caterpillar climbing a stalk. The caterpillar fell but climbed again — and again — until it reached the top. Inspired by its perseverance, Yared returned to his studies with renewed dedication. He mastered the scriptures and became a deacon in the Ethiopian Orthodox Church. One day, while praying, Yared received divine inspiration and created the five-note scale system (zema) that became the foundation of Ethiopian liturgical music. He composed the Deggua (hymn book) and invented musical notation for the Ge'ez language. Three birds — representing the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — are said to have guided his voice, and his music was so beautiful that angels descended from heaven to listen.
source: Ethiopian Orthodox tradition, hagiography of Saint Yared
moral: Perseverance in the face of difficulty leads to divine inspiration and great achievement.

source_references.title: A History of Ethiopia, Volume I: Northern Ethiopia and the Kingdom of Aksum
source_references.author: E.A. Wallis Budge

title: The Sultan of Pate and the Liongo Proclamation
summary: When Oromo emissaries came to visit the Sultan of Pate, they heard tales of the legendary hero Liongo. Curious, they asked the Sultan about him. The Sultan sent for Liongo, who packed his bag and hung three great horns about his waist. The journey that took the messengers two days, Liongo completed in half a day. Arriving at the edge of town, he blew his first horn with such force that it split in two. The courtiers were startled, but the Sultan recognized the blast: Liongo had arrived. A second horn blast at the city gates, and a third before the palace announced his presence. When Liongo entered the court, his immense height and strength awed the Oromo visitors. He recited a praise poem celebrating his bow, the Song of the Warrior (Sifu Uta Wangu), and the Oromo delegates left with new respect for the Swahili kingdom. This story illustrates Liongo's role as the protector and symbol of Swahili identity.
source: Swahili oral tradition, Alice Werner's Myths and Legends of the Bantu
moral: True strength announces itself without need for boasting.

source_references.title: Four Centuries of Swahili Verse: A Literary History and Anthology
source_references.author: Jan Knappert

title: Simbi: The River Spirits of the Kongo
summary: The Simbi are powerful water spirits who inhabit the rivers, streams, and sacred pools of the Kongo region. They are ancient beings who existed before humanity and witnessed the creation of the world. Simbi can appear as beautiful human figures, snakes, or shimmering reflections on the water's surface. They are guardians of esoteric knowledge and the natural balance of the forests and waters. Those who respect the Simbi and make proper offerings may receive visions, healing, or supernatural abilities. But those who pollute the waters or hunt excessively incur the Simbi's wrath — they may be dragged into the depths, never to be seen again, or afflicted with madness. The nganga (spiritual specialists) serve as intermediaries between the human world and the Simbi, conducting ceremonies at sacred river sites. The Simbi remind the Kongo people that water is not merely a resource but a living, spiritual force that sustains all existence.
source: Kongo oral tradition, Kongo religion studies
moral: The natural world is animated by spirits who must be respected and honored.

source_references.title: Flash of the Spirit: African & Afro-American Art & Philosophy
source_references.author: Robert Farris Thompson

title: The Sacred Drum of the Luba Realm
summary: According to Luba tradition, the first sacred drum was given to the people by the spirit of the forest. It was said to have the voice of the ancestors, and its beat could be heard across the entire kingdom. The drum contained the soul of the Luba nation — as long as it beat, the kingdom would endure. The Luba kings (mulopwe) kept the sacred drum in a special house guarded by initiated priests. The drum was played only during coronations, royal funerals, and times of great crisis. Its rhythm was a coded language that announced the king's health, the coming of war, or the death of a ruler. When the Belgians colonized the Congo, the last sacred drum was hidden in the forest to prevent its capture. Some say it still beats deep in the Katanga forest, preserving the spiritual heartbeat of the Luba kingdom. The drum represents the continuity of Luba royal power and the connection between the living rulers and their ancestral predecessors.
source: Luba oral tradition, Central African folklore
moral: A nation's spirit cannot be conquered as long as its sacred traditions endure.

source_references.title: The Royal Arts of Africa: The Majesty of Form
source_references.author: Suzanne Preston Blier

title: The Spirit of Kilimanjaro
summary: A Chaga legend tells of a poor hunter named Mushi who lost his way in the dense forest on Kilimanjaro's slopes. After days of wandering, starving and exhausted, Mushi came upon a clearing where an old woman sat beside a fire. The old woman was the spirit of the mountain, though Mushi did not know it. She gave him food and shelter, asking only that he not look back when he left at dawn. In the morning, Mushi walked away without looking back, and found himself at his own village in an instant, his pack heavy with blessings. When others heard of his fortune, a greedy man from a neighboring village climbed the mountain seeking the same favor. The old woman also fed him, but his greed got the better of him and he looked back before leaving, reducing his blessings to ash. The mountain spirit teaches that those who approach Kilimanjaro with humility and gratitude receive its bounty, while the greedy and disrespectful are left with nothing.
moral: Rewards come to those who are grateful and humble, not to the greedy.

title: The Sacred Groves of the Chaga
summary: The Chaga people maintain sacred groves (mshitu) on the slopes of Kilimanjaro, patches of virgin forest that are never cut or farmed. According to tradition, these groves are the dwelling places of the mountain spirits (warimu) who protect the Chaga people. The largest and most sacred grove is said to be where Ruwa himself first touched the mountain. Within these groves, the trees are never felled, the animals are never hunted, and only initiated elders may enter. The groves serve as the burial grounds for chiefs (Mangi) and as sites for the most important ceremonies. When a child is born, a tree is planted in the family's grove; when a person dies, their spirit returns to the grove to join the ancestors. The Mangi (chief) is responsible for ensuring the groves remain untouched. The Chaga believe that if the sacred groves are ever destroyed, Ruwa's protection will leave Kilimanjaro and the mountain's glaciers will finally melt, ending the Chaga people's special bond with the great mountain.
source: Chaga oral tradition, Sally Falk Moore 'The Chagga and Meru of Tanzania'
moral: Protecting sacred natural spaces preserves the spiritual and physical health of the community.

title: The Six Initiation Societies of the Bambara
summary: Bambara spiritual life is organized through six initiatory societies: N'domo, Komo, Nama, Kono, Tyiwara, and Kore. Each society represents a stage of knowledge and spiritual development, guiding members from childhood through full spiritual maturity. The Komo society, one of the most powerful, preserves esoteric knowledge about the cosmos, medicine, and divination. The N'domo society deals with circumcision and the transition to manhood. The Kore society represents the highest level of initiation, conferring 'mastery of speech' and complete understanding of cosmic order. Together these societies provide the complete intellectual, moral, and religious education of the Bambara people, with ceremonies held at key agricultural and astronomical intervals.

source_references.title: Les Fondements de la Société Dogon
source_references.author: Germaine Dieterlen

title: Silamaka and Pullori: The Fulani Epic Heroes
summary: The Fulani epic of Silamaka and Pullori recounts the adventures of two bound-by-friendship heroes from the Macina region of Mali. Silamaka, the saber-wielding warrior, is challenged to prove his temerity against the mighty Bambara king Da Monzon Diarra. He consults a geomancer who tells him that to become invulnerable, he must capture alive the sacred serpent Galamani that prowls the forest. With Pullori's help, Silamaka succeeds where many had failed, capturing the serpent and fashioning a protective belt. His fame spreads across Fulani villages. Da Monzon counterattacks with powerful magic, but Pullori, armed with Silamaka's enchanted lance, flees with their sons. As night falls, they all disappear, ascending to heaven. The epic symbolizes Fulani resistance against Bambara domination and remains a cornerstone of Fulani oral tradition.

source_references.title: A Treasury of African Folklore: The Oral Literature, Traditions, Myths, Legends, Epics, Tales, Recollections, Wisdom, Sayings, and Humor of Africa
source_references.author: Harold Courlander

title: Sina and the Eel (Origin of the Coconut)
summary: The beautiful maiden Sina drew water from a pool where she discovered a young eel. She took it home as a pet and named it Tuna. The eel grew rapidly, outgrowing bowls, then pools, and becoming unnaturally large. Wherever Sina bathed, the eel appeared and encircled her. Frightened, she fled across multiple islands, but the eel always found her. Finally, the eel revealed itself as the King of Fiji transformed by magic, who confessed his love and asked Sina to bury his head. Though shocked, Sina complied. From the buried head grew the first coconut tree, its three dark pores representing the eel's eyes and mouth. When cracked, the coconut's shell resembles the eel's face.

title: The Seven Sisters (Pleiades) Dreaming
summary: The Seven Sisters is a widespread Aboriginal Australian Dreaming story found across many language groups. The story tells of seven sisters who fled across the land and into the sky to escape a persistent pursuer, often a man named Wurrurrumbi or a hunter who wanted to take one of the sisters as his wife. As they fled, the sisters created landmarks, waterholes, and important sites across the Australian landscape. Finally they leaped into the sky, becoming the Pleiades star cluster. The pursuing man became the star Orion or the Moon, still chasing them across the heavens. In different regional versions, the sisters are associated with the arrival of the monsoon season or with ceremonies for girls' initiation. The story encodes practical knowledge about seasonal changes and the movements of celestial bodies crucial for survival.

title: The Story of Ablai Khan
summary: Ablai Khan (1711-1781) was one of the greatest Kazakh khans, who united the three Kazakh zhuzes (hordes) and led the struggle against the Dzungar invaders. According to legend, as a thirteen-year-old orphan named Abulmansur, he worked as a shepherd disguised as a beggar called 'Sabalak' to avoid his father's rivals. His destiny was revealed when he miraculously defeated a Dzungar champion in single combat, earning the title 'batyr' (hero). He was a master diplomat who played Russia against China to maintain Kazakh independence. Legend says he was blessed by the spirit of his ancestor Genghis Khan and commanded the loyalty of all the steppe tribes through a combination of military genius and sacred charisma.

title: Semetey: The Son of Manas
summary: After Manas's death, his treacherous younger brothers Abyke and Bobosh seized power and tried to kill Manas's son Semetey. Kanykey, Manas's widow, escaped with the infant Semetey to Bukhara, where her father ruled as khan. Semetey grew up unaware of his heritage until his mother revealed his identity. He returned to the Talas valley on the magical horse Taytoru to avenge his father's death. Semetey defeated his treacherous uncles in battle and beheaded the forty companions of his father who had betrayed Manas. He married the beautiful Ay-Chürök, a swan maiden, and continued his father's legacy of defending the Kyrgyz people. The second part of the Manas trilogy bears his name.

source_references.title: The Manas of Wilhelm Radloff
source_references.author: A.T. Hatto
source_references.publisher: In International Folkloristics, Rowman & Littlefield, Lanham

title: Shurale the Forest Spirit
